questions.question: A 40-year-old male presents with shortness of breath. He also has lower back pain and stiffness of the spine. A chest radiograph shows bilateral upper-zone fibrosis with elevation of the hila. Spinal ligamentous ossification is also noted. High-resolution CT shows peripheral interstitial changes with traction bronchiectasis and paraseptal emphysematous changes in the upper zones. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Pulmonary changes occur in ankylosing spondylitis in 1% of patients, with reticulonodular abnormality in the upper zones progressing to confluent opacification and fibrosis. Ossification of spinal ligaments is typical in ankylosing spondylitis.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most likely finding in a 56-year-old female patient with shortness of breath where a high-resolution CT scan shows mosaic perfusion with no air trapping on expiratory scan?
questions.detail: Mosaic perfusion with no air trapping on expiratory scans suggests vascular obstruction such as chronic thromboembolic disease.

questions.question: A 28-year-old male with known Marfan’s syndrome presents with chest pain and shortness of breath. What are the most likely echocardiogram findings?
choices: C. aortic stenosis and post-stenotic dilatation
questions.detail: Marfan's syndrome often features myxomatous degeneration of the aortic wall, leading to dilatation of the aortic root and aortic regurgitation.

questions.question: Into which structure does the thoracic duct normally drain?
choices: E. junction of left subclavian and internal jugular veins
questions.detail: The thoracic duct typically drains into the junction of the left subclavian and internal jugular veins.

questions.question: A 28-year-old female undergoes uterine artery embolization for a large fibroid. What is the commonest complication occurring in the first 12 months following this procedure?
choices: B. failure of therapy with need for re-embolization or hysterectomy
C. hysterectomy for uterine infection or pain
questions.detail: Persistent non-offensive vaginal discharge is the most common complication in the first 12 months following uterine artery embolization.

questions.question: A 55-year-old presents with chest pain and bilateral, symmetrical, hilar adenopathy. Which feature on cardiac MRI would make cardiac sarcoid a more likely diagnosis than ischaemia?
choices: A. delayed hyperenhancement of lateral wall
B. full-thickness, abnormal, high T2 signal in lateral wall
D. partial-thickness, abnormal, high T2 signal with subendocardial sparing at base of septum
E. segmental area of reduced enhancement in lateral wall on early phase study
questions.detail: Abnormal high T2 signal with subendocardial sparing at the base of the septum is more characteristic of cardiac sarcoid.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the radiographic changes seen in acute rejection of a lung transplant?
choices: A. mosaic perfusion and air trapping
B. pleural effusion and septal thickening with no left ventricular dysfunction
C. bilateral consolidation at the bases
E. globular heart and bat-wing perihilar consolidation
questions.detail: Acute rejection typically presents with pleural effusion and septal thickening without left ventricular dysfunction.

questions.question: An 87-year-old male with fever and cough has dense consolidation in the right mid-zone with a bulging oblique fissure. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Klebsiella pneumonia is characteristically associated with dense consolidation and a bulging fissure.

questions.question: A 76-year-old female has a right upper lobe mass containing a crescent of air. Which feature on CT would make a cavitating malignancy more likely than aspergilloma?
questions.detail: In cavitating malignancy, the central mass often enhances and is fixed, whereas aspergillomas are mobile and do not typically enhance.

questions.question: A 42-year-old male with nosebleeds and renal impairment has multiple cavitary lesions predominantly in the lower lobes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Wegener's granulomatosis often presents with upper respiratory tract symptoms, renal impairment, and cavitary lung lesions.

questions.question: A 50-year-old male with known chronic asthma has a routine chest radiograph. Which feature is most likely to be seen?
questions.detail: In an asymptomatic patient with chronic asthma, the chest radiograph is most likely to be normal.

questions.question: A 65-year-old man has symmetrical periosteal reaction in the wrists and ankles and a pleurally based mass. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: A. hypertrophic osteoarthropathy with bronchogenic carcinoma
B. hypertrophic osteoarthropathy with malignant mesothelioma
C. hypertrophic osteoarthropathy with pleural fibroma
questions.detail: Hypertrophic osteoarthropathy associated with a localized, enhancing, pleurally based mass is characteristic of a pleural fibroma.

questions.question: A right-sided aortic arch with mirror-image branching is most frequently associated with which congenital cardiac abnormality?
choices: A. pulmonary atresia and ventricular septal defect
E. corrected transposition of the great vessels
questions.detail: Fallot’s tetralogy is the most common association for a right-sided aortic arch with mirror-image branching.

questions.question: A 68-year-old man with asbestos exposure has bilateral pleural thickening and a mass with curved bronchovascular markings coursing towards the hilum. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: The 'comet tail sign' of curved bronchovascular markings associated with pleural thickening is diagnostic of rounded atelectasis.

questions.question: A 43-year-old female with Churg–Strauss syndrome has a high-resolution CT. What is the most likely finding?
questions.detail: Churg–Strauss syndrome typically shows centrilobular nodules and ground-glass opacities on HRCT.

questions.question: A 32-year-old male with weak lower limb pulses and a 'figure-3' indentation of the aorta has which diagnosis?
choices: E. transposition of the great vessels
questions.detail: The 'figure-3' sign on chest radiograph and inferior rib notching are classic for coarctation of the aorta.

questions.question: A 25-year-old male with recurrent epistaxis and red vascular skin blemishes has lung opacities with bands extending to the hila. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Epistaxis, skin blemishes, and pulmonary arteriovenous malformations are classic features of HHT (Osler–Weber–Rendu).

questions.question: From which vessel does the single right bronchial artery usually arise?
choices: B. joint origin with left bronchial arteries
C. second posterior right intercostal artery
D. third posterior right intercostal artery
E. fourth posterior right intercostal artery
questions.detail: The solitary right bronchial artery usually arises from the third posterior right intercostal artery.

questions.question: A patient with polyarteritis nodosa has acute loin pain. What is most likely to be seen on ultrasound?
choices: B. a solid mass with a perinephric collection
C. multiple renal artery aneurysms and perinephric collection
E. small kidneys with increased echogenicity
questions.detail: Polyarteritis nodosa characteristically causes multiple small intrarenal aneurysms, which may rupture to cause a perinephric collection.

questions.question: A 5-year-old with intermittent epigastric discomfort has transverse colon protruding through a defect anteriorly in a parasternal position. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: A Morgagni hernia is an anteromedial developmental defect, most commonly occurring on the right side.

questions.question: A 45-year-old with allergies and malaise has resolving peripheral consolidation on chest radiographs and eosinophilia. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Migratory or transient peripheral consolidation with eosinophilia is classic for Loeffler’s syndrome.

questions.question: Following blunt trauma, which finding at CT is most suggestive of tracheal injury?
choices: D. focal overdistension of endotracheal tube cuff
questions.detail: Focal overdistension of the endotracheal tube cuff (ballooning) is a highly suggestive CT sign of a tracheal tear.

questions.question: On an FDG PET scan, which of the following focal uptake sites is least concerning for malignancy?
choices: C. diffuse uptake in caecal wall
questions.detail: While focal muscle uptake can be concerning, it often has benign causes; however, symmetrical tonsillar or diffuse caecal uptake is typically physiological.

questions.question: A 42-year-old with tuberous sclerosis has a large perinephric haematoma 48 hours after embolization of a 7 cm renal angiomyolipoma. What is the cause?
choices: B. spontaneous haemorrhage from non-embolized lesion
questions.detail: Post-embolization rupture is a recognized, albeit rare, complication of large renal AML treatment.

questions.question: In ventilation–perfusion scintigraphy, which finding is suggestive of an intermediate probability of pulmonary embolism?
choices: A. matched non-segmental defects with a normal chest radiograph
B. multiple unmatched small perfusion defects with normal ventilation
C. large, segmental, matched defect with similar-sized opacity on chest radiograph
E. two large, unmatched, segmental, perfusion defects
questions.detail: Large matched segmental defects with corresponding radiographic opacities are categorized as intermediate probability.

questions.question: A 78-year-old with superior vena caval syndrome has an irregular superior mediastinal mass. There is no respiratory compromise. What is the most appropriate next step?
choices: D. stenting of superior vena cava
questions.detail: In the absence of life-threatening complications, obtaining a tissue diagnosis is the priority to guide specific therapy.

questions.question: A 65-year-old male with a history of tuberculosis has facial swelling and an irregular paratracheal calcified mass compressing the SVC. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Fibrosing mediastinitis is often secondary to TB and characteristically causes a calcified mass that compresses mediastinal structures.

questions.question: A chest radiograph shows fine calcification Overland the cardiac silhouette adjacent to the left sternal edge at the 4th intercostal space. What is the cause?
questions.detail: The location is consistent with mitral valve calcification, which is most commonly due to rheumatic heart disease.

questions.question: A 37-year-old male feels unwell the day after smoke inhalation. What is the most likely chest radiograph finding now?
questions.detail: Pulmonary oedema often develops 12–24 hours after smoke inhalation injury.

questions.question: A 40-year-old female with low IQ and seizures has extensive honeycombing and normal lung volumes. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Lymphangiomyomatosis (LAM) is associated with tuberous sclerosis and presents with thin-walled cysts/honeycombing and normal or high lung volumes.

questions.question: A 28-year-old male involved in an RTA has tracheal shift to the right, depression of the left main bronchus, and loss of aortic knuckle clarity. What diagnosis should be considered?
questions.detail: These radiographic signs are classic for a traumatic aortic rupture/haematoma.

questions.question: A 27-year-old with sickle cell disease has a right-sided lobulated paravertebral mass with widened rib spaces. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Extramedullary haematopoiesis, seen in chronic anaemias like sickle cell, often presents as paraspinal masses.

questions.question: A 50-year-old with dyspnoea has 'bat-wing' consolidation and 'crazy-paving' on CT. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis is the classic cause of a 'crazy-paving' pattern on CT.

questions.question: A 56-year-old male has a mass within the right middle lobe bronchus with distal collapse. Which feature would make hamartoma more likely than carcinoid?
questions.detail: The presence of fat within a well-defined endobronchial or peripheral mass is diagnostic of hamartoma.

questions.question: A 52-year-old female on dialysis has upper-lobe-predominant airspace-type opacities with calcification and chest wall vessel calcification. What is the cause?
questions.detail: Metastatic pulmonary calcification is seen in chronic renal failure, typically in an upper lobe distribution.

questions.question: A 75-year-old former mine worker has nodules and a sausage-shaped mass paralleling the mediastinum. What is the diagnosis?
choices: A. coal worker’s pneumoconiosis with bronchogenic carcinoma
B. coal worker’s pneumoconiosis with progressive massive fibrosis
D. primary lung carcinoma with metastases
questions.detail: Progressive massive fibrosis (PMF) often presents as sausage-shaped masses in the upper zones in pneumoconiosis patients.

questions.question: A 25-year-old male with childhood lung infections has a hyperlucent left lung with reduced perfusion and delayed ventilation washout. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Macleod's (or Swyer–James) syndrome is an acquired condition following childhood bronchiolitis obliterans, presenting with a hyperlucent lung.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most typical description of an atrial myxoma?
choices: A. left atrial mass, no atrial enlargement, pulmonary oedema
B. right atrial mass, enlarged right atrium, clear lungs
C. left atrial mass, enlarged left atrium, calcified lung nodules, pulmonary oedema
D. right atrial mass, enlarged right atrium, pulmonary oedema
E. left atrial mass, dilated SVC, IVC and azygos vein
questions.detail: Left atrial myxomas typically cause obstruction leading to left atrial enlargement and pulmonary venous congestion.

questions.question: A 58-year-old male with a paravertebral mass that is fatty but has calcification and soft-tissue enhancement likely has which diagnosis?
questions.detail: Soft-tissue components and enhancement within a fatty mass favor liposarcoma over a simple lipoma.

questions.question: In patients with rheumatoid arthritis, what is the commonest pulmonary finding seen on the chest radiograph?
questions.detail: Pleural disease, particularly effusions or thickening, is the most common lung manifestation of RA.

questions.question: A smooth, tubular opacity adjacent to the right heart border is seen in a 35-year-old male. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: E. partial anomalous pulmonary venous return
questions.detail: The 'scimitar sign' describes an anomalous pulmonary vein draining into the IVC or RA, appearing as a tubular opacity.

questions.question: In multidetector CT angiography, which technique reduces the effects of calcification?
choices: E. digital subtraction of pre- and post-contrast studies
questions.detail: Digital subtraction allows the removal of calcified plaque from the final angiographic images.

questions.question: A 52-year-old male with a raised left hemidiaphragm showing paradoxical movement likely has which condition?
questions.detail: Phrenic nerve palsy, causing paradoxical movement, is often due to invasion by mediastinal malignancy.

questions.question: A 6 cm abdominal aortic aneurysm with high T1 signal in mural thrombus and no perianeurysmal fluid requires what management?
choices: D. routine referral to vascular surgeon
E. emergency assessment by vascular surgeon
questions.detail: Intramural haemorrhage (high T1 signal) within an aneurysm is an unstable sign requiring urgent surgical evaluation.

questions.question: A 44-year-old male has a 20 mm solitary pulmonary nodule with a central cavity and smooth internal walls. What investigation is needed?
choices: A. none; findings are entirely benign
questions.detail: Solitary pulmonary nodules over 10 mm require further evaluation, with PET/CT being a common next step.

questions.question: The vessel supplying the sinoatrial node most commonly arises from which structure?
choices: E. left coronary artery main stem
questions.detail: The sinoatrial node artery typically arises from the RCA in about 60% of individuals.

questions.question: In cardiac MRI for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, mass is best assessed in which plane?
questions.detail: The short axis oblique plane is standard for assessing left ventricular muscle mass.

questions.question: Which Doppler ultrasound feature suggests occlusion of the right brachiocephalic vein?
choices: A. collapse of right internal jugular vein on sniffing
B. variation of flow with respiration in right subclavian vein
C. variation with cardiac cycle in right subclavian vein
D. continuous monophasic flow in the right subclavian vein
E. collapse of the left internal jugular vein on sniffing
questions.detail: Continuous monophasic flow with loss of cardiac and respiratory variation indicates a more central venous obstruction.

questions.question: A superior mediastinal mass with tracheal narrowing and displacement in a 43-year-old female is likely what?
choices: C. aneurysm of the aortic arch
questions.detail: Superior mediastinal masses with calcification and tracheal displacement are most commonly thyroid goitres.

questions.question: Which PET/CT technique helps characterize a 2 cm rounded mass in the lung apex using SUV?
choices: A. maximum SUV corrected for lean body mass
B. maximum SUV corrected for body weight
D. dual time point assessment of SUV
E. assessment of SUV centrally and peripherally
questions.detail: Dual time point imaging, measuring SUV at 60 and 120 minutes, can help differentiate malignancy from inflammation.

questions.question: A 20-year-old with a pericarinal mass of 10 HU likely has which diagnosis?
questions.detail: Bronchogenic cysts are typically found in a pericarinal location and show fluid attenuation (0–10 HU).

questions.question: A 16-year-old with a pneumothorax and calcified lung nodules likely has metastases from what?
questions.detail: Osteosarcoma characteristically presents with calcified pulmonary metastases and can cause spontaneous pneumothorax.

questions.question: A right hilar mass with ipsilateral reticulation, beaded septal thickening, and Kerley lines suggests what?
questions.detail: Unilateral lymphangitis carcinomatosis is almost always due to bronchogenic carcinoma.

questions.question: A 38-year-old male with thin-walled cysts and centrilobular nodules in the lungs likely has what?
questions.detail: Langerhans cell histiocytosis characteristically presents with small nodules that progress to thin-walled cysts.

questions.question: In patients undergoing lung resection, which imaging investigation is the best predictor of postoperative function?
questions.detail: Ventilation scintigraphy is considered the best predictor of postoperative FEV1.

questions.question: A patient with rib fractures develops a rounded opacity with an air–fluid level after initial consolidation resolves. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: A pulmonary laceration can fill with blood or air, presenting as a post-traumatic cyst (pneumatocele/haematocele).

questions.question: A 52-year-old, 1-year post-heart transplant, has multiple lung nodules and hilar adenopathy. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: PTLD is a recognized complication of chronic immunosuppression and often presents with nodules and adenopathy.

questions.question: A 48-year-old female has a rounded 2 cm hepatic artery lesion with arterial flow following a biopsy. What is the treatment?
choices: C. transcatheter placement of covered stent
questions.detail: Visceral pseudoaneurysms, particularly those that are iatrogenic, are typically treated with coil embolization.

questions.question: Which lung segments are separated by the superior accessory fissure?
choices: A. apical segment of lower lobes from other lower lobe segments
B. apical segment of right upper lobe
D. lingular segment of upper lobe
questions.detail: The superior accessory fissure separates the apical segment of the lower lobe from the basal segments.

questions.question: In normal anatomy, which vascular structure lies most anteriorly posterior to the manubrium?
questions.detail: The left brachiocephalic vein is the most anterior vascular structure at the thoracic inlet.

questions.question: Which description is most suggestive of a pulmonary hamartoma?
choices: A. round 2 cm central mass with no fat
B. irregular 8 cm mass with cavitation
D. lobulated 3 cm mass with calcification and fat in the periphery
E. 5 cm peripheral lesion with connecting bands
questions.detail: Hamartomas are typically peripheral and often contain fat or 'popcorn' calcification.

questions.question: In persistent left-sided SVC, where does drainage usually occur?
questions.detail: Persistent left SVC most commonly drains into the coronary sinus.

questions.question: An 18-year-old with dextrocardia, polyposis, and bronchiectasis should have which further investigation?
questions.detail: Kartagener’s syndrome features this triad and is associated with male infertility due to sperm dysmotility.

questions.question: A mass between the aortic arch and pulmonary artery may cause which presentation?
choices: D. swelling of face and neck
questions.detail: The recurrent laryngeal nerve passes through the aortopulmonary window and its compression causes hoarseness.

questions.question: A 65-year-old male with asthma needs myocardial perfusion imaging but cannot exercise. Which agent should be used?
questions.detail: Dobutamine is the preferred stress agent in patients with reactive airway disease like asthma.

questions.question: After EVAR, a sac increase is seen with contrast in the periphery not contacting the stent. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Type II endoleaks arise from collateral vessels (e.g., IMA/lumbar arteries) and contrast is seen in the sac periphery.

questions.question: In thoracic lymphoma, which feature would favour Non-Hodgkin over Hodgkin disease?
questions.detail: Hodgkin's rarely involves the posterior mediastinal nodes, whereas NHL is more common in this location.

questions.question: Which structure lies immediately anterior to the left main bronchus at the hilum?
questions.detail: The left superior pulmonary vein is the most anterior structure in the left hilum.

questions.question: Which feature on CT makes restrictive cardiomyopathy more likely than constrictive pericarditis?
questions.detail: Pericardial thickness > 4 mm is characteristic of constrictive pericarditis, while normal thickness points to RCM.

questions.question: Which structure forms the right mediastinal border along with the right atrium and SVC?
questions.detail: In adults, the right border is formed by the right brachiocephalic vein, SVC, and right atrium.

questions.question: In bronchopulmonary sequestration, which feature suggests intralobar over extralobar?
choices: B. no connection to bronchial tree
questions.detail: Intralobar sequestrations are enclosed within the same visceral pleura as the rest of the lung.

questions.question: In a mediastinal teratoma, which feature suggests it is benign?
choices: E. mass projecting from both sides
questions.detail: Calcification, especially in the form of a tooth, is suggestive of a benign nature in teratomas.

questions.question: A 24-hour post-embolization patient has loin pain, fever, and gas in a non-enhancing renal tumor. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Post-embolization syndrome is common after renal artery embolization and can cause fever, pain, and gas in necrotic tissue.

questions.question: Which feature would suggest sarcoidosis over rheumatoid lung?
questions.detail: Sarcoidosis typically shows a mid-to-upper-zone predominance, while RA usually affects the bases.

questions.question: What is the uterine radiation dose for CTPA relative to V/Q scintigraphy?
questions.detail: CTPA has a higher total body dose but a lower uterine dose compared to V/Q scanning.

questions.question: Which appearance is most in keeping with pulmonary hydatid disease?
choices: C. solitary mass with air–fluid level and floating debris
E. 3 cm mass with central calcification
questions.detail: Ruptured hydatid cysts characteristically show an air-fluid level and the 'water-lily sign' of floating membranes.

questions.question: A 56-year-old female has clavicle erosion, rib notching, and narrowed humeral–acromial space. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Rheumatoid arthritis can cause erosion of the lateral clavicles and superior rib notching.

questions.question: In the left lower lobe, which bronchi share a common origin?
questions.detail: On the left side, the anterior basal and medial basal segmental bronchi usually share a common trunk.

questions.question: A 35-year-old female smoker has multiple bilateral renal artery stenoses. What is the treatment?
questions.detail: Angioplasty is the treatment of choice for fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) with a high success rate.

questions.question: On HRCT, how can mosaic perfusion be distinguished from ground-glass opacification?
choices: A. opaque areas have small vessels
B. lucent areas have small vessels
C. mosaic is only lower zones
questions.detail: In mosaic perfusion, the lucent (oligaemic) areas have smaller caliber vessels compared to the denser areas.

questions.question: A 59-year-old with arrhythmia has high-density consolidation at the lung base. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Amiodarone contains iodine, which can cause high-density consolidation or nodules in the lungs.

questions.question: Which feature on HRCT makes silicosis more likely than sarcoidosis?
questions.detail: PMF sausage-shaped masses in the upper zones are characteristic of silicosis or coal worker's pneumoconiosis.

questions.question: A 25-year-old in renal failure has perihilar consolidation and subsequent progression to an interstitial pattern. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Goodpasture’s syndrome is defined by pulmonary haemorrhage (perihilar consolidation) and renal failure.

questions.question: What are the common cardiac manifestations in Marfan’s syndrome?
questions.detail: Myomatous degeneration in Marfan's leads to aortic root dilatation and regurgitation.

questions.question: What is the commonest pulmonary finding in RA?
questions.detail: Unilateral or bilateral pleural effusion is the most frequent finding in rheumatoid lung disease.

questions.question: In which plane is muscle mass best assessed for HCM in cardiac MRI?
questions.detail: Short axis oblique is standard for LV mass calculation.

questions.question: A continuous monophasic flow in the subclavian vein on US Doppler suggests what?
questions.detail: Loss of phasic variation in central veins suggests proximal obstruction.

questions.question: What is the best predictor of postoperative function in lung resection?
questions.detail: Ventilation scintigraphy is superior for predicting postoperative FEV1.

questions.question: A sausage-shaped mass paralleling the mediastinum in a miner is classic for what?
questions.detail: PMF masses are a classic feature of pneumoconiosis in dust-exposed workers.

questions.question: What is the standard treatment for Young Adult renal artery stenosis (FMD)?
questions.detail: Angioplasty is the gold standard for treating FMD-related renal stenosis.

questions.question: What does a 'crazy-paving' HRCT pattern most strongly suggest?
questions.detail: Crazy-paving is a classic description for the appearances of pulmonary alveolar proteinosis.

questions.question: An intensely enhancing pericarinal cyst is most likely what?
questions.detail: Bronchogenic cysts are typically located near the carina.

questions.question: Which complication is most frequent after UAE for fibroids?
questions.detail: Non-offensive vaginal discharge is the leading long-term complication post-UAE.

questions.question: Where is the 'figure-3' sign localized in aortic coarctation?
questions.detail: The 'figure-3' sign represents narrowing at the site of coarctation, typically near the ductus arteriosus.

questions.question: A right-sided mass between segments T8–12 in an anaemic patient is what?
questions.detail: Extramedullary haematopoiesis produces characteristic paraspinal masses in anaemic patients.

questions.question: What is the 'scimitar sign' associated with?
questions.detail: The scimitar sign is a feature of anomalous pulmonary venous return draining to the IVC.

questions.question: What is the most anterior structure at the thoracic inlet?
questions.detail: The left brachiocephalic vein is the most anterior vascular structure at this level.

questions.question: Calcification in a Tooth-shape in a mediastinal mass is what?
questions.detail: Benign teratomas often contain specific calcified elements like teeth.

questions.question: Ahyperlucent lung with air trapping post-infection is what syndrome?
questions.detail: Swyer-James/Macleod syndrome presents as hyperlucency post-bronchiolitis obliterans.

questions.question: Which artery most commonly feeds a juvenile angiofibroma?
questions.detail: The internal maxillary artery is the primary blood supply for JNAs.
